coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible carbon dots from fermented and non-fermented coconut water: Evaluation of antioxidant, antimitotic, antibacterial and cytotoxic activities
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Anugraha, S.N.
Sabira, O.
Ajaykumar, A.P.
Reshmi, C.P.
Leeja, L.
Nataraj, D.
Ramesh, A.R.
coredata.dc:description: A green, economical, microwave-assisted synthesis was used to prepare fluorescent, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) from fermented and non-fermented coconut water and compared their multifunctional bioactivities, including antioxidant, antimitotic, antibacterial, and anticancer properties. Characterization using HR-TEM, XRD, FTIR and Raman spectroscopy confirmed successful CD formation, with fermentation-induced modification enhances their physicochemical properties. Antioxidant assays demonstrated that fermented CDs (FCW-CDs) exhibited a 65 % higher radical scavenging activity than non-fermented CDs (NFCW-CDs). Antibacterial tests showed significant inhibition against K. pneumoniae and S. aureus, with fermented CDs displaying greater zone of inhibition. In antimitotic studies, fermented CDs showed no chromosomal aberrations, whereas non-fermented CDs induced negligible aberrations. This highlights the superior biocompatibility of fermented CDs. The DLA cell assay confirmed that fermented CDs exhibited 1.45 times higher cytotoxicity compared to non-fermented CDs. These findings suggest that fermentation enhances the bioactive properties of coconut water derived CDs, making them promising candidates for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Biomass
Fermented coconut water
Antibacterial activity
Antioxidant activity
Antimitotic activity
Cytotoxic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Illustration of carbon dot synthesis from non-fermented/fermented coconut water.
(a) X-ray diffraction pattern of FCW-CDs and (b) NFCW-CDs.
(a) The D and G bands of the Raman spectrum of FCW-CDs and (b) NFCW-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of FCW-CDs and (b) NFCW-CDs.

UV–vis absorption spectrum, and emission spectrum at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm of (a) FCW-CDs and (b) NFCW-CDs. The inset shows photographs of the corresponding CD solutions under daylight and UV light.
Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) FCW-CDs and (b) NFCW-CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 nm to 410 nm with increments of 10 nm.
Fluorescence decay curve of FCW-CDs.
(a) HR-TEM image and (b) lattice fringes of FCW-CDs. The inset of (b) shows the SAED pattern.
Standard inhibition curve of (a) ascorbic acid, (b) FCW-CDs and (c) NFCW-CDs.

Schematic illustration of the dual role of carbon dots as prooxidants and antioxidants. Under visible/normal light irradiation they generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) as prooxidants and neutralize ROS as antioxidants, converting them back to oxygen (3O2) and water (H2O).
In vitro antibacterial analysis of FCW-CDs on (a) K. pneumoniae and (b) S. aureus.
In vitro antibacterial analysis of NFCW-CDs on (a) K. pneumoniae and (b) S. aureus.

Schematic representation of the interaction between carbon dots and a bacterial cell under normal light. CDs generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to oxidative stress, DNA damage, and bacterial membrane disruption.
(a) In vitro cytotoxicity activity of FCW-CDs and (b) NFCW-CDs.

Schematic illustration of carbon dots (CDs) interacting with tumor cells, showing cellular uptake, light-triggered accumulation, reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, and subsequent cell lysis and death.

The effect of the FCW-CDs on dividing A. cepa cells. (A) Normal prophase (Control), (B) normal prophase (CDs), (C) late anaphase (Control), (D) late anaphase (CDs), (E) anaphase (Control) and (F) anaphase (CDs).

The effect of the NFCW-CDs on dividing A. cepa cells. (A) Normal prophase (Control), (B) disintegrated prophase (CDs), (C) normal metaphase (Control), (D) abnormal metaphase (CDs), (E) normal anaphase (Control) and (F) laggard anaphase (CDs).
Illustration of carbon dots (CDs) interacting with cells undergoing normal mitotic division, depicting the sequential stages of prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, and cytokinesis.
Comparison of the quantum yields of carbon dots reported in previous studies.
OD values of ascorbic acid, FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs at 700 nm.
Inhibition % of ascorbic acid, FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs.
IC50 value of ascorbic acid, FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs.
Antibacterial activity (zone of inhibition, mm) of FCW-CDs against K. pneumonia and S. aureus.
Antibacterial activity (zone of inhibition, mm) of NFCW-CDs against K. pneumonia and S. aureus.
Comparison of carbon dots, silver nanoparticle and chitosan-based hydrogel.
Mitotic Index of control and FCW-CDs on A. cepa cells.
Mitotic index of control and NFCW-CDs on A. cepa cells.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible carbon dots from fermented and non-fermented coconut water: Evaluation of antioxidant, antimitotic, antibacterial and cytotoxic activities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green, economical, microwave-assisted synthesis was used to prepare fluorescent, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) from fermented and non-fermented coconut water and compared their multifunctional bioactivities, including antioxidant, antimitotic, antibacterial, and anticancer properties. Characterization using HR-TEM, XRD, FTIR and Raman spectroscopy confirmed successful CD formation, with fermentation-induced modification enhances their physicochemical properties. Antioxidant assays demonstrated that fermented CDs (FCW-CDs) exhibited a 65 % higher radical scavenging activity than non-fermented CDs (NFCW-CDs). Antibacterial tests showed significant inhibition against K. pneumoniae and S. aureus, with fermented CDs displaying greater zone of inhibition. In antimitotic studies, fermented CDs showed no chromosomal aberrations, whereas non-fermented CDs induced negligible aberrations. This highlights the superior biocompatibility of fermented CDs. The DLA cell assay confirmed that fermented CDs exhibited 1.45 times higher cytotoxicity compared to non-fermented CDs. These findings suggest that fermentation enhances the bioactive properties of coconut water derived CDs, making them promising candidates for biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent nano carbons with unique physicochemical and optical properties ((Liu et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2019; Wang and Hu, 2014). Since their discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), CDs have gained research interest due to their low-cost synthesis, tunable luminescence, water solubility, high stability, biocompatibility and low toxicity compared to quantum dots. Their diverse applications include drug delivery, sensing, bio imaging, antibacterial, solar cells, optoelectronics and catalysis (Aryamol et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024b; L. Cui et al., 2021; Nair et al., 2023; Nangan et al., 2023; Ramasubburayan et al., 2024, Ramasubburayan et al., 2023; Thirumalaivasan et al., 2024; V.A. Ansi et al., 2021; Wang and Hu, 2014). Both top-down and bottom-up methods, including laser ablation, oxidation-reduction, pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment, are used for CD synthesis (Wang and Hu, 2014). However, many are limited by complexity, time consumption and the need for strong chemicals and costly equipment (Pundi and Chang, 2022). Green synthesis, focusing on renewable feedstocks and safer chemicals, has recently gained attention (Chahal et al., 2021).

One of the best carbon sources for the green synthesis of CDs is eco-friendly biomass. A diverse range of biomass sources, including various plant parts like peels, fruits, seeds and husks have been explored for the synthesis of CDs (Liao et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2024). These biomass sources are green, abundant in nature, easy to obtain, inexpensive and renewable sources of carbon. Additionally, most of these biomass sources contain heteroatoms and eliminate the need for the addition of external heteroatoms during the preparation of CDs (Arul et al., 2023; Sharma et al., 2024).

Coconut (Cocos nucifera L.) water, a naturally occurring beverage, is composed of over 95 % water content and the remaining 5 % comprising carbohydrates (glucose, fructose, sucrose, sorbitol), amino acids (lysine, histidine, tryptophan), organic acids (tartaric, citric, malic) and metals (potassium, calcium) (J.W·H Yong et al., 2009). Recently, several research groups have synthesized CDs from coconut water using acid-assisted ultrasonic method, stirrer-assisted heating method, pressure-free hydrothermal method and microwave-assisted method (Aly et al., 2024; Jayan et al., 2021; Manoharan et al., 2020; Murugesan et al., 2020; Nugroho et al., 2022; Preethi et al., 2021; Purbia and Paria, 2016). These CDs have shown promising applications in sensing various analytes. For instance, Ponpandian and coworkers used coconut water derived CDs for Fe3+ ions detection by monitoring the quenching of fluorescence intensity of the CDs (Preethi et al., 2021). Benchawattananon and coworkers demonstrated the use of luminol-added coconut water CDs for latent fingerprint detection (Nugroho et al., 2022). Paria and coworkers explored the use of tender coconut water CDs as “turn on” fluorescent sensors for the detection of thiamine (Purbia and Paria, 2016). Notably, sensing of aniline was also achieved by non-synthetic luminescent graphene quantum dots extracted from coconut water (Aly et al., 2024). However, the use of coconut water derived CDs for biological applications remain relatively unexplored, presenting an exciting opportunity for further research.

In this study, we demonstrate a simple, environmentally friendly green synthesis of highly luminescent CDs from fermented and non-fermented coconut water using microwave synthesis. Coconut water contains a significant amount of sugars as well as carbon and nitrogenous compounds. Fermentation by microorganisms induces various chemical changes in coconut water. During yeast fermentation, a range of volatile compounds are produced, including esters, higher alcohols, aldehydes and ketones. Since coconut water have limited sugars, added sugar provides fermentable carbohydrates, promoting microbial activity and ensuring efficient production of alcohol or acids. It also helps regulate fermentation and improves product consistency (Zhang et al., 2018). While several reports describe CD preparation from coconut water, it appears that no study has explored CDs derived from fermented coconut water. We utilized non-fermented and fermented system to synthesize highly fluorescent non-fermented coconut water carbon dots (NFCW-CDs) and fermented coconut water carbon dots (FCW-CDs) using a domestic microwave oven. Subsequently, these NFCW-CDs and FCW-CDs were evaluated for their antioxidant, antimitotic, antibacterial, and cytotoxic activities.

The ingredients such as coconut, sugar, baker's yeast were purchased from a local supermarket (Vadavalli, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India). Urea (CH4N2O, 99 %) and Potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6], 99 %) were procured from spectrum reagents and chemicals pvt. Ltd. sodium dodecyl sulfate (C12H25NaO4S, 99.5 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 99.9 %), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 98.5 %) and ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, 99 %) were obtained from nice chemicals. Dalton's lymphoma ascites (DLA) cell line test was done at Amala Cancer Research Centre, Thrissur, Kerala. Trypan blue (C34H24N6Na4O14S4, 99.5 %), Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and Quinine sulfate (C40H50N4O8S, 99 %) were procured from Merck Ltd. Allium cepa L. obtained from local vegetable market, Pattambi, Kerala.

FCW-CDs were synthesized from fermented coconut water. 110 mL of coconut water was filtered through a muslin cloth, and added 1.5 g of sugar and 0.5 g of yeast. The solution is mixed well, transferred into a glass vessel, covered with muslin cloth and kept for 24 h to get fermented coconut water. To 50 mL of non-fermented/fermented coconut water, 1 g urea was added and heated in a domestic microwave oven at 190 °C for about 5 min to get NFCW-CDs/FCW-CDs. The yellowish-brown paste was diluted to a final volume of 15 mL using water and subjected to multiple rounds of centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 15 min to get the supernatant. To get rid of tiny molecules and oligomers, (Hinterberger et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2024; Ullal et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2021) the solution was dialysed against ultrapure water in a dialysis bag with MW cut-offs of 1000 Da for 24 h. The resulting NFCW-CDs/FCW-CDs were further concentrated to 5 mL. Fig. 1 shows the synthesis schematic diagram of NFCW-CDs/FCW-CDs.

The preliminary identification of obtained carbon dots fluorescence was identified using a Rotek UV cabinet. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs were recorded using T90 UV–Visible spectrometer and Fluro Max-4 L spectrofluorometer, M/s. Horiba Instruments, USA. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected using empyrean Malvern Panalytical diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation in the scan range of 2θ, 20–80°. The morphological and microstructural properties were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using a Jeol/JEM 2100 operating at 200 kV. Functional groups were verified using FTIR spectroscopy on a Jasco FT/IR-6800 type A, and spectra were collected from 500 to 4000 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy measurements were carried out using WiTec alpha 300, Germany.

The ferric ion reducing power (FRAP assay) was measured by the method developed by Pulido et al. (Pulido et al., 2000). In ferric ion reducing antioxidant power method, a total of 0.1 mL of the FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs were separately mixed with 0.9 mL 96 % C2H5OH, 5 mL water, 1.5 mL 1 M hydrochloric acid, 1.5 mL 1 % K3[Fe(CN)6], 0.5 mL 1 % sodium dodecyl sulfate and 0.2 % FeCl3 in a water bath maintained at 50 °C for 20 min. It was then allowed to cool and the optical density (OD) at 700 nm is measured. Ascorbic acid served as a positive control. Percentage inhibition was determined using the formula:
Inhibition
%
= 1 −
A
A 1
x
100
Where A and A1 are the absorbance of the blank and the sample, respectively.

The IC50 value, determined from the regression equation y = mx + c, represents the amount of FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs needed to achieve 50 % inhibition of free radical activity. Lower IC50 values indicate greater antioxidant potency of the FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs.

An in vitro cytotoxicity assay was performed using Dalton's Lymphoma Ascites (DLA), which were isolated from the peritoneal cavity of tumor-bearing mice. Cells were washed thrice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and counted using the trypan blue exclusion method to determine cell viability (Talwar, 1974). A suspension of 1 × 106 viable cells in 0.1 mL was incubated separately with various concentrations of FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs in a final volume of 1 mL for 3 h at 37 °C. A control containing only cells was included. Following incubation, 0.1 mL of 1 % trypan blue was added, and the mixture was incubated for 2–3 min before cell counting using a hemocytometer. The percentage cytotoxicity was calculated as follows:
Percentage of cytotoxicity =
No . of dead c ells
No . of live cells + No . of dead cells
x
100

The antibacterial activities of FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs were distinctly evaluated in vitro against both Klebsiella pneumoniae (Gram-negative bacteria) and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacteria) using the agar disc diffusion assay method (Mathew et al., 2022). Bacterial strains were applied to Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) plates using a sterilized cotton swab. For the assay, a sterile blank antimicrobial susceptibility disc was used. Three different concentrations of FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs (5, 10, and 15 μL) were applied to the discs, with ampicillin as the positive control and 20 μL each of fermented coconut water and non-fermented coconut water as controls. The entire system was incubated at 37 °C for 16 h. The antibacterial activity determined by measuring the zones of inhibition around the disc.

This study examined the effects of biosynthesized FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs on mitosis at concentrations ranging from 100 μL to 500 μL using fresh Allium cepa (onion) bulbs. Roots were carefully excised from the root primordia disc, and a squashing method was utilized to create a thin cell film (Sharma and Sharma, 1990). Around 1400 cells from the top three preparations were meticulously analyzed for mitotic activity with a Leica camera (LEICA ICC50E). The Mitotic Index (%MI) was subsequently calculated to assess the impact of CDs on cell division. This comprehensive analysis provided valuable insights into the potential genotoxic and cytotoxic effects of CDs on plant root cells. The precise preparation and scoring methods ensured the accuracy and reliability of data concerning mitotic activity in treated Allium cepa cells, contributing to a deeper understanding of the biological impact of CDs at the cellular level.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern ( Fig. 2a ) of the synthesized FCW-CDs exhibits a single broad peak around 21.9°, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of carbon materials with a high degree of sp3 disorder. The interlayer spacing of FCW-CDs is calculated to be 0.405 nm, indicating poor crystallinity and the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (Ramanan et al., 2016). Whereas, the XRD pattern of NFCW-CDs ( Fig. 2b) shows a broad peak around 23.6°, with a reduced interlayer spacing of 0.37 nm, suggesting improved structural ordering compared to FCW-CDs. This difference in interlayer spacing and peak position highlights the variations in crystallinity and functional group distribution between FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs.

The Raman spectrum of the FCW-CDs ( Fig. 3a ) displays D band at 1340 cm−1 and G band at 1540 cm−1. The G band is attributed to the in-plane vibrational mode of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. On the other hand, the D band corresponds to vibrational modes associated with disordered graphitic carbon structures containing dangling bonds. The ID/IG ratio, calculated from the Raman spectrum, is a measure of disorder and sp3/sp2 carbon ratio. In the present case, the ratio of ID/IG is determined to be 0.94, indicating moderately high degree of defects and functional groups within the carbon structure (Ma et al., 2016). Similarly, the Raman spectrum of NFCW-CDs ( Fig. 3b) shows a D band at 1343 cm−1 and a G band at 1560 cm−1, with an ID/IG ratio of 0.87. The lower ID/IG ratio of NFCW-CDs compared to FCW-CDs suggests that NFCW-CDs have relatively fewer structural defects and functional groups, indicating a more ordered carbon structure. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of FCW-CDs ( Fig. 4a ) reveals the occurrence of key functional groups, including OH, NH (broad peak around 3300 cm−1) and CO (1636 cm−1). Similarly, the FTIR spectrum of NFCW-CDs ( Fig. 4b) shows a broad peak at 3292 cm−1, attributed to OH and NH stretching, and a peak at 1639 cm−1, indicating CO stretching. This observation suggests the availability of hydroxyl/phenol, amine and carbonyl moieties of both FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs structure, potentially useful for various biological applications (Baydar and Baydar, 2013).

Microwave-assisted synthesis from fermented and nonfermented coconut water successfully yielded biocompatible, fluorescent, water-soluble carbon dots (FCW-CDs & NFCW-CDs) with a fluorescence quantum yield of 33 % and 12.1 % respectively. Both FCW-CDs and NFCW-CDs demonstrated promising potential for diverse applications due to their antioxidant properties, selective cytotoxicity towards DLA tumor cells, and antibacterial activity against Klebsiella pneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus. Additionally, onion bulbs exposed to various concentrations of FCW-CDs showing no genotoxic or mitodepressive effects and NFCW-CDs showing negligible mitodepressive effects. FCW-CDs demonstrated enhanced biocompatibility, superior fluorescence properties, and improved antioxidant, antimicrobial, antimitotic and anticancer activities compared to NFCW-CDs influenced by the fermentation process. The study highlights the potential of fermentation in improving the biomedical efficacy of coconut-water derived CDs, making them suitable candidates for sustainable biomedical and environmental applications.

S.N. Anugraha: Methodology, Investigation. O. Sabira: Resources. A.P. Ajaykumar: Resources. C.P. Reshmi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Visualization. L. Leeja: Resources. D. Nataraj: Resources. A.R. Ramesh: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.N.A., C.P.R. and A.R.R. thank DST-FIST (award number: SR/FST/325/2016) for the funding. The authors acknowledge Amala Cancer Research Centre, Thrissur for DLA cell line test.
